geographic_scope: Subnational coastal corridor and camp belt from Rashidiyyeh/Tyre through Sidon, with Nabatiyeh-Rihan pockets.
theater_description: Fast-moving invasion front through the main Palestinian population and force concentrations in southern Lebanon, especially the coastal camps and cities around Tyre and Sidon and the Nabatiyeh-Rihan zone.
geometry_method_note: Best represented as a corridor-plus-nodes theater: Tyre/Rashidiyyeh, Burj al-Shamali/al-Buss, Ayn al-Hilwa/Sidon, and Nabatiyeh-Rihan. The geometry changed rapidly during the first weeks of the invasion.
military_area_note: PLO deployment was concentrated around the coastal camps and cities and the Nabatiyeh-Rihan area, but units fragmented quickly after the first days.
civilian_area_note: Explicit refugee counts exist for the camps and southern refugee population, but not for all Lebanese civilians physically present in the whole exposed corridor.
split_reason: The initial invasion front differs sharply from the later static siege of West Beirut in geography, force structure, and source architecture.
quality_note: Good fragmentary source coverage for Palestinian camp populations and PLO deployment, but the full all-civilian exposed denominator for the south remains too weak for a defensible core DR.

theater_description: Static siege of encircled West Beirut after the Israeli advance reached the capital and cut off the western half of the city.
geometry_method_note: Use the encircled West Beirut urban footprint as of 14 June 1982, excluding East Beirut. This is the clearest city-level denominator footprint in the period.
military_area_note: Military exposure covers organized armed personnel inside the West Beirut perimeter, primarily PLO fighters and, in upper scenarios, some PLA/Syrian personnel remaining in the theater.
civilian_area_note: Civilian exposure covers civilians physically present in encircled West Beirut, adjusted downward for documented flight of 30,000-60,000 people.
split_reason: This episode has the strongest denominator footprint: explicit trapped civilian counts, fighter counts, and a discrete start/end bounded by encirclement and evacuation.
quality_note: Good triangulation on the theater footprint and several casualty series, but the civilian/military split is partly derived and inflicting-side attribution is not cleanly separable across the full episode.

geographic_scope: West Beirut departure corridors, port, and supervised evacuation routes.
theater_description: Negotiated evacuation of PLO, PLA, and Syrian personnel from West Beirut under multinational supervision.
geometry_method_note: Micro-corridors linked to port and land exits rather than a civilian exposure polygon.
military_area_note: Military counts are strong for departing armed personnel, but exposure time is brief and civilian harm series are not well assembled for this narrow phase.
civilian_area_note: Remaining civilians in West Beirut are known only approximately and are not cleanly separated from the immediately preceding siege episode.
split_reason: Violence mode changes from siege to negotiated withdrawal; denominator logic changes from trapped population to transit/extraction.
quality_note: Excellent evacuation counts exist, but not a correspondingly clear civilian death series for this short phase.

geographic_scope: Micro-urban Sabra neighborhood and Shatila camp in southwest Beirut.
theater_description: Post-evacuation massacre in Sabra and the adjacent Shatila camp after Israeli re-entry into West Beirut and the entry of Phalangist units into the camps.
geometry_method_note: Micro-theater consisting of Sabra and the adjacent Shatila camp; Kahan gives camp dimensions but not a robust same-period exposure census.
military_area_note: Combatant exposure inside the camps is not reliably measurable after the PLO evacuation, making the military denominator effectively unresolved for DR purposes.
civilian_area_note: Civilian victimization is well documented, but the exposed resident count is only loosely bounded by tertiary and later camp-profile sources.
split_reason: Perpetrator structure, violence mode, and moral-legal character change completely after the siege and evacuation.
quality_note: Numerator attribution is strong, but the denominator and combatant-exposure side of the DR are too weak, producing a censored or undefined military rate.
